A: Patient with history of symptomatic partial epilepsy, static encephalopathy, cerebral palsy, shunted hydrocephalus, and factor V Leiden deficiency presenting with increased lethargy and recent seizure activity.

S: The patient is an 80-year-old African-American male with a history of asbestos exposure and extensive cardiac disease. He presents with worsening shortness of breath, constipation, cough, and hemoptysis (blood mixed with sputum). He denies fever.

O:
Vital Signs: BP 136/80, HR 70, RR 20, Temp 99.3, SpO2 96% on 2L NC.
General: Elderly male, awake, alert, oriented, no acute distress.
HEENT: Peripheral cyanosis.
Neck: Supple.
Lungs: Bibasilar crackles with decreased breath sounds in the left base.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm with murmur and metallic click.
Abdomen: Soft and benign.
Extremities: 1+ cyanosis, no clubbing, no edema.
Labs: WBC 6.9, Hgb 10.6, Hct 31.2, Plt 160,000. CK 266, PTT 37, PT 34, INR 3.7. Na 141, K 4.2, Cl 111, CO2 23, BUN 18, Cr 1.7, Glu 91, Ca 8.6, Total Protein 6.1, Albumin 3.3, Total Bilirubin 1.

A: 80-year-old male with extensive cardiac history (including mechanical aortic valve, ICD, CHF with EF 40%, CAD, AFib) and asbestos exposure presenting with hemoptysis, worsening shortness of breath, and constipation. Physical exam reveals bibasilar crackles, decreased breath sounds, and peripheral cyanosis. Labs show elevated INR (3.7) and mild anemia.

P: Admit patient for management of hemoptysis, worsening shortness of breath, and constipation. Continue current medication regimen including Coumadin, Prevacid, Diovan, Flomax, Coreg, Aldactone, Lasix, and Zocor. Monitor respiratory status and cardiac function.
label: h200-nvl/qwen3.6-35b-a3b/soap_en

A: Pubic cellulitis.

P: Continue current antibiotic regimen (Rocephin and Cleocin). Monitor blood culture results.

S: The patient reports feeling much better today with improved weakness and decreased muscular pain. He has no other complaints.

O: General: Not in acute distress, awake, alert and oriented x3. Vitals: BP 147/68, HR 82, RR 20, Temp 97.7, O2 sat 99% on 3 L. HEENT: NC/T, PERRLA, EOMI. Neck: Supple. Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. Respiratory: Clear bilateral. Abdomen: Soft and nontender. Extremities: No edema, pulses present bilateral. Labs: Total CK 200 (down from 25,573, previously 70,142), Troponin 2.3 (up from 1.9), BNP pending, Blood sugar 93, BUN 55.7, Creatinine 2.7, Sodium 137, Potassium 3.9, Chloride 108, CO2 22. LFTs: AST 704, ALT 298, Alk Phos 67, Total Bilirubin 0.3. CBC: WBC 9.1, Hgb 9.9, Hct 29.2, Plt 204. Blood cultures pending. Imaging: Abdominal ultrasound negative, kidneys echogenic with cortices suggesting chronic medical renal disease. Lower extremity Doppler negative for DVT.

A: 1. Rhabdomyolysis, likely secondary to statins/gemfibrozil (discontinued on admission). 2. Acute on chronic renal failure. 3. Anemia, likely hemodilutional. 4. Leukocytosis, improving. 5. Elevated liver enzymes, likely secondary to rhabdomyolysis. 6. Hypertension, controlled. 7. Elevated cardiac enzymes. 8. Obesity. 9. DVT prophylaxis.

P: 1. Continue IV fluids and monitor rhabdomyolysis. 2. Follow up with Nephrology recommendations for renal failure. 3. Repeat CBC in the morning for anemia. 4. Follow up with Cardiology recommendations for elevated cardiac enzymes. 5. Continue Lovenox 40 mg subcutaneously daily for DVT prophylaxis.

O: VITAL SIGNS: Temperature is 95.4. Highest temperature recorded over the past 24 hours is 102.1. CHEST: Examination of the chest is clear to auscultation. CARDIOVASCULAR: First and second heart sounds were heard. No murmurs appreciated. ABDOMEN: Benign. Right renal angle is tender. Bowel sounds are positive. EXTREMITIES: There is no swelling. NEUROLOGIC: The patient is alert and oriented x3. Examination is nonfocal. LABORATORY DATA: White count is down from 35,000 to 15.5. Hemoglobin is 9.5, hematocrit is 30, and platelets are 269,000. BUN is down to 22, creatinine is within normal limits.
A: 1. Sepsis due to urinary tract infection. Urine culture shows Escherichia coli, resistant to Levaquin. 2. Urinary tract infection. 3. Hypotension. Resolved. 4. Ischemic cardiomyopathy. No evidence of decompensation. 5. Diabetes type 2. Uncontrolled. 6. Recent pulmonary embolism, INR is above therapeutic range. 7. History of coronary artery disease. Troponin indeterminate. Most likely troponin is secondary to impaired clearance.
P: 1. Sepsis due to urinary tract infection: Changed to doripenem. 2. Urinary tract infection: Treat with doripenem, change Foley catheter. 3. Hypotension: Continue intravenous fluids. 4. Ischemic cardiomyopathy: Monitor. 5. Diabetes type 2: Continue insulin sliding scale. 6. Recent pulmonary embolism: Coumadin is on hold, monitor. 7. History of coronary artery disease: Continue medical treatment.

S: 12-year-old male presents for healthy checkup and sports physical. Mother reports he is "little bit congested at times" and has been told he is allergic to grasses; over-the-counter Claritin helps but he continues to sniffle. Patient reports dryness on his face and asks for a cream recommendation. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Current medications include Claritin p.r.n. No known drug allergies. Family and social history are unremarkable. Diet consists of whole milk and a variety of foods. Growth chart reviewed. Voids and stools well. In seventh grade, participating in cross-country and track. Needs to wear glasses but was not wearing them today; vision was not tested as he recently saw an eye doctor, though the need for glasses was discussed.

O: General: Well-developed, well-nourished male in no acute distress. Dermatologic: Without rash or lesion. HEENT: Head normocephalic and atraumatic. Eyes: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact. Red reflexes present bilaterally. Optic discs sharp with normal vasculature. Ears: Tympanic membranes gray, translucent with normal light reflex. Nares very congested. Turbinates swollen and boggy. Neck: Supple without masses. Chest: Clear to auscultation and percussion, easy respirations, no accessory muscle use. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, heaves, or gallops. Back: Symmetric, no scoliosis or kyphosis, normal flexibility. Femoral pulses 2+ and symmetric. Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended, no hepatosplenomegaly. GU: Normal Tanner III male, testes descended bilaterally. Hearing normal. Speech normal.

A: 12-year-old male in no acute distress. Allergic rhinitis symptoms (congestion, swollen/boggy turbinates) likely related to grass allergy. Facial dryness noted. Vision correction needed but not currently worn. Otherwise healthy development and physical exam.

P: Continue Claritin p.r.n. for allergy symptoms. Recommend moisturizing cream for facial dryness. Counsel on importance of wearing prescribed glasses for vision. Encourage participation in sports (cross-country/track). Routine follow-up as needed.

S: 46-year-old male with Down’s syndrome presents with mother for follow-up of hypothyroidism and onychomycosis. He completed six weeks of Lamisil without issues. O: Weight 149 lbs (up 2 lbs). BP 120/80. Pulse 80 regular. Neck supple, no adenopathy, thyromegaly, or nodules. Cardiac regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Toenails remain thickened and yellowed with no change.

P: 1. Recheck ALT and TSH today and call results. 2. Prescribe Lamisil 250 mg #30 one p.o. daily with one refill to complete next eight weeks of therapy if ALT is normal; reviewed symptoms of liver dysfunction. 3. Continue Levothroid 0.1 mg daily unless dosage adjustment needed based on TSH.

S: The patient presents for right ureteroscopy, Holmium laser lithotripsy, and right ureteral stent placement due to a right distal ureteral calculus. Past medical history includes prostatism and coronary artery disease. Past surgical history includes right spermatocelectomy, left total knee replacement in 1987, and right knee surgery in 2005. Medications include Coumadin 3 mg daily, Fosamax, and Viagra p.r.n. Allergies are none. Review of systems is negative for shortness of breath, chest pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, weakness, or strokes; patient voids well. Family history is noncontributory.

O: General appearance is an alert male in no distress. HEENT is grossly normal. Neck is supple. Lungs are clear. Heart shows normal sinus rhythm with no murmur or gallop. Abdomen is soft with no masses. Genitalia shows normal penis and bilaterally descended testicles. Rectal examination is benign. Extremities show no edema.

A: Right distal ureteral calculus.

P: Right ureteroscopy, ureteral lithotripsy. Risks and complications discussed with the patient. Patient signed true informed consent. No guarantees or warrantees were given.

S: 53-year-old right-handed male presents for evaluation of diplopia. Symptoms began in January 2009 upon waking; initially resolved within one hour but recurred the next day. Over the following week, intensity and frequency worsened, becoming severe by the second week. Current symptoms include constant diplopia with objects appearing closer together horizontally compared to the initial wide horizontal separation. Patient does not drive or work due to symptoms. Reports no temporal fluctuation. Recently developed right supraorbital pain, described as pain under the right lid. Denies dysphagia, dysarthria, weakness, numbness, tingling, or other neurological symptoms.

O: Patient was seen by a primary care physician and referred to the emergency department for urgent MRI to evaluate for possible aneurysm. Neurology consultation occurred on February 2, 2009. MRI showed a questionable 3 mm aneurysm of the medial left supraclinoid internal carotid artery; no abnormalities on the right side. MRV was negative. MRI of the brain with and without contrast was negative.

A: Diplopia, initially episodic and now constant, with associated right supraorbital pain. Questionable 3 mm aneurysm of the medial left supraclinoid internal carotid artery noted on prior imaging.

P: Further evaluation of diplopia and right supraorbital pain. Review of prior imaging findings regarding questionable aneurysm.
label: h200-nvl/qwen3.6-35b-a3b/soap_en

A: Acute gastroenteritis or alcohol-induced gastritis.

S: 71-year-old female presents with a 7- to 8-day history of persistent abdominal pain. She was seen at ABC ER 3-4 days ago, had a "normal" CT scan, and was discharged on oral Cipro and Flagyl. She denies nausea, vomiting, fevers, chills, jaundice, bright red blood per rectum, melena, or significant recent weight loss. She reports persistent anorexia, passes flatus, but had obstipation symptoms with her last bowel movement two days ago. Her last colonoscopy was 5 years ago. Past medical history includes atrial fibrillation (now in normal sinus rhythm on metoprolol) and hormone replacement with Premarin. Past surgical history includes cholecystectomy, appendectomy, and hysterectomy. She has a known grade 4 bladder prolapse. She is allergic to Macrobid. She denies smoking or alcohol use. Review of systems is negative for recent febrile illnesses, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

O: General: Elderly thin white female, pleasant, in no acute distress. Vital signs: Temperature 98.8°F, all other vital signs stable and within normal limits. HEENT: Head normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae non-injected. Neck: Supple. Chest: Clear. Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. Abdomen: Generally nondistended and soft.

A: Persistent abdominal pain for 7-8 days despite prior ER evaluation, normal CT scan, and antibiotic therapy. History of atrial fibrillation, grade 4 bladder prolapse, and prior abdominal surgeries.

P: Continue current management. Monitor symptoms. Consider further evaluation given persistent pain and history of obstipation.
label: h200-nvl/qwen3.6-35b-a3b/soap_en

A: Nausea and abdominal pain of uncertain etiology. Secondary diagnosis of migraine headache. Complications: None. Discharge condition: Guarded.

P: Follow up with physician in the office in 5 to 7 days to resume all pre-admission medications. Diet and activity as tolerated.

A: 26-year-old G2P1-0-0-1 at 28-1/7 weeks gestation presenting with left lower quadrant pain, constipation, nausea, vomiting, and urinary retention.

P: Continue current management for pain and nausea. Monitor fetal status and maternal vital signs. Address constipation and urinary retention. Review substance use history and drug screen results.

S: 26-year-old G2P1-0-0-1 at 28-1/7 weeks presents with left lower quadrant pain, no bowel movement for two weeks, nausea, and vomiting for the last 24 hours. She reports not voiding in the last 24 hours due to pain. She denies leaking of fluid, vaginal bleeding, or uterine contractions. She reports good fetal movement and denies fevers, chills, or burning with urination. Review of systems is positive for lower back pain. Mother reports patient eats without difficulty but continues to yell out requesting pain medication and complaining of severe pain.

O: Vitals: BP 139/82, HR 89, RR 20, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 96°F. Fetal heart tones are 130s with moderate long-term variability. Past medical history includes irritable bowel syndrome and three urinary tract infections (patient unsure if pyelonephritis was present). Past surgical history is negative. Allergies: NKDA. Medications: Phenergan and Zofran twice daily; Macrobid use questionable. GYN history includes abnormal Pap and Group B Strep status within normal limits; denies STDs. OB history: G1 term SVD without complications (6-year-old); current care at Lyndhurst. Social history: Denies tobacco and alcohol; endorses marijuana use and history of cocaine use five years ago. Lab systems show multiple positive urine drug screens, including recent positives for benzodiazepines, barbiturates, opiates, and marijuana (February 2008) and cocaine (2005).

A: 26-year-old G2P1-0-0-1 at 28-1/7 weeks gestation presenting with left lower quadrant pain, constipation, nausea, vomiting, and urinary retention.

P: Continue current management for pain and nausea. Monitor fetal status and maternal vital signs. Address constipation and urinary retention. Review substance use history and drug screen results.

S: Patient presents for follow-up of immune thrombocytopenia. Platelets dropped to 10 on 01/09/07, prompting an increase in prednisone to 60 mg daily. Repeat labs on 01/16/07 showed platelets increased to 43. Patient reports no bleeding problems. Hematologist recommends decreasing prednisone to 40 mg for 3 days, then to 20 mg daily. Patient had previously been on 20 mg every other day with platelets hovering above 20.

O: Vitals as in chart. Patient is alert, pleasant, and cooperative, and in no apparent distress. Petechial areas on legs have resolved.

A: Patient shows improvement in platelet count following a burst of prednisone.

P: Decrease prednisone to 40 mg for 3 days, then to 20 mg daily. May attempt to further reduce dosage over time. Continue osteoporosis medications including bisphosphonate, calcium, and vitamin D. Arrange for weekly CBC draws.

S: The patient is a 41-year-old African-American male previously well known to the provider. He presents at the request of Dr. X for management of anticoagulation and heart disease following surgery for attempted nephrolithotomy. He experienced significant postoperative bleeding, necessitating the discontinuation of all anticoagulation. He reports no chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, or dizziness. He is married, lives with his wife, works as a barber, and does not use alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs.

O: Past medical history includes aortic valve disease status post aortic valve replacement on 10/15/2007, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, congestive heart failure, transient ischemic attack with no residual neurologic deficits, Type I diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, morbid obesity, sleep apnea syndrome, and status post thyroidectomy for thyroid carcinoma. Family history is negative for coronary artery disease; mother has congestive heart failure. Review of systems is unremarkable except for genitourinary issues related to recent surgery. Medications prior to admission included Clonidine, Atenolol, Simvastatin, Furosemide, Metformin, Hydralazine, Diovan, Lisinopril, Amlodipine, Lantus insulin, KCl, NovoLog sliding scale insulin, Warfarin, and Levothyroxine.

A: The patient has a history of aortic valve disease status post replacement, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and congestive heart failure, which were stable prior to this admission. He is currently experiencing significant postoperative bleeding following nephrolithotomy, requiring the cessation of anticoagulation therapy.

P: Management of anticoagulation is required due to postoperative bleeding. Management of his underlying heart disease, including aortic valve disease, atrial fibrillation, and congestive heart failure, is also indicated.
label: h200-nvl/qwen3.6-35b-a3b/soap_en

S: Mr. ABC, a 2-year-old boy, presents with complaints of belly and back pain, though he remains active and is eating and drinking well. Parents report no changes in urine or bowel habits, no fever, chills, cough, congestion, earache, or sore throat. They note a foul odor from the right nose and suspect a retained foreign body, recalling a previous removal of a piece of cotton a few weeks ago. The mother restarted a remaining course of antibiotics. Social history reveals parents smoke around the house.

O: Vital signs are stable and the patient is afebrile. General examination reveals a well-nourished, well-developed, pleasant, and cooperative 2-year-old male in no acute distress, appearing healthy and nontoxic. HEENT exam shows normal tympanic membranes and canals. The left naris is normal. The right naris exhibits a foul odor and questionable purulent drainage.

A: 2-year-old male with suspected retained foreign body in the right nose presenting with foul odor and purulent drainage, accompanied by abdominal and back pain.

P: Continue management for suspected retained foreign body and associated infection. Monitor abdominal and back pain.
label: h200-nvl/qwen3.6-35b-a3b/soap_en
